title: NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit
exclusions: Does not decide who may access systems, approve joiner-mover-leaver tickets, interpret Article 21 national transpositions, or replace lawyer-reviewed access policies. It only aggregates technical log evidence.
evidence: Scheduled saved search `UC-22.2.5` CSV/PDF export, supporting raw-event samples in `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search`, and RFC 3161 timestamp metadata from `evidenceSigning` stored beside quarterly NIS2 control packets.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(i) requires human resources security, access control policies, and asset management. The UC correlates authentication patterns across Windows, Entra ID, Okta and CyberArk to surface weak logon types, after-hours elevation, and identity sprawl. Access policy authorship and HR vetting remain outside Splunk, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.5: NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.5' running on index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" (EventCode, Account_Name, Logon_Type, Workstation_Name, Status, dest), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Synthetic fixture seeds `index=windows` with `EventCode=4672`, `Account_Name=test_admin`, `after_hours=1`, and matching `index=pam` `cyberark:session` rows for the same user within five minutes; the saved search returns at least one combined stats row with `auth_result=special_privileges` and non-zero count, proving cross-system correlation.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Fixture contains only `nis2_service_accounts.csv` listed users performing scripted Logon Type 4 backups during the maintenance window with `Workstation_Name` allow-listed; the production alert child search applying the lookup filter returns zero actionable rows while still logging a green health metric.

dataSources: `index=windows` `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security` and `index=auth` with `sourcetype=azure:aad:signin` or `sourcetype=okta:log`, plus `index=pam` `sourcetype=cyberark:session` for CyberArk Privileged Session Manager (Splunkbase 2891). Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services ([Splunkbase 3110](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3110)) for Entra ID / Graph modular inputs. Extracted fields for auditors include EventCode, Account_Name, Logon_Type, userPrincipalName, authenticationRequirement, dest, and session_id.
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (Splunkbase 2891)
spl:
(index=windows sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN (4624, 4625, 4672)) OR (index=auth sourcetype IN ("azure:aad:signin","okta:log")) OR (index=pam sourcetype="cyberark:session") earliest=-24h
| eval user=coalesce(Account_Name, userPrincipalName, user, Username)
| eval auth_result=case(EventCode=4624,"success", EventCode=4625,"failure", EventCode=4672,"special_privileges", match(lower(_raw),"(?i)success"),"success", match(lower(_raw),"(?i)fail|denied"),"failure", 1=1,"other")
| eval after_hours=if(tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))<7 OR tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))>19, 1, 0)
| stats count by sourcetype, EventCode, auth_result, user, dest, Logon_Type, after_hours
| sort -count
description: Correlates interactive sign-in success and failure, privilege-use signals, and privileged-session telemetry across Windows domain assets, Entra ID, Okta, and CyberArk vault sessions so reviewers can spot weak logon types, after-hours elevation, and cross-system identity sprawl affecting essential services.
value: Regulators and internal audit teams need a defensible narrative that access to network and information systems is logged, attributable, and reviewable without assembling spreadsheets from five admin portals. This pattern concentrates those signals into one scheduled artefact with timestamps suitable for year-end NIS2 evidence packs.
implementation: (1) Deploy Splunk Add-on for Windows (742) with Security log collection; enable Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110) Graph sign-in inputs; enable Okta (6553) and CyberArk (2891) inputs for `index=auth` / `index=pam`. (2) In `authorize.conf`, grant the compliance role `srchIndexesAllowed` for `windows`, `auth`, `pam`. (3) Maintain `nis2_service_accounts.csv` and `nis2_access_review_exceptions.csv` lookups. (4) Schedule daily; archive PDF/CSV to `index=audit_evidence`. (5) Optional: accelerate the CIM Authentication model for executive roll-ups.
visualization: Timechart of failed logons (4625), table of after-hours 4672 events, stacked bar of auth_result by sourcetype, single value of unknown users exceeding threshold.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110)

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Microsoft Entra ID CA report-only policies flooding success rows** during a Conditional Access migration. *Distinguish:* `authenticationRequirement` remains `singleFactorAuthentication` while **Entra ID > Monitoring > Sign-ins** shows policy in report-only. *Suppress:* time-bound exception rows in `nis2_access_review_exceptions.csv` with `exception_valid_until`. 2) **CyberArk dual sessions during password rotation** where the same admin reconnects within minutes. *Distinguish:* consecutive `session_id` values from `sourcetype=cyberark:session` share one `ticket_id`. *Suppress:* filter `where mvcount(session_id)<2` after `stats` or maintain an allow list lookup keyed on `ticket_id`. 3) **Okta service accounts triggering "failure" strings in `_raw`** from harmless API 429 retries. *Distinguish:* `actor.displayName` matches `nis2_service_accounts.csv`. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_service_accounts username AS user OUTPUT is_service | where isnull(is_service)`. 4) **Backup operators running night jobs with Logon Type 4** appearing as after-hours privilege risk. *Distinguish:* `Workstation_Name` matches backup fleet prefix. *Suppress:* `where NOT match(Workstation_Name,"(?i)^BK-")`. 5) **Domain controllers forwarding cross-domain authentication** inflating user cardinality. *Distinguish:* `dest` resolves to DC FQDN patterns per Active Directory Sites. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_dc_inventory dest OUTPUT is_dc | where isnull(is_dc) OR auth_result!="success"`.
equipmentModels: CyberArk Privileged Access Security (PAM session and vault audit logs)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110, Entra ID sign-in and audit logs)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, user provisioning and RBAC data)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Authentication data model and Assets & Identities)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `windows`, `auth`, `pam` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(i)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.5: NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `windows`, `auth`, `pam` under `WinEventLog:Security`, `cyberark:session`, `azure:aad:signin`, `okta:log`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=windows earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


9 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Deploy Splunk Add-on for Windows. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** with Security log collection; enable Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Graph sign-in inputs; enable Okta. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** and CyberArk. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** inputs for `index=auth` / `index=pam`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 6:** In `authorize.conf`, grant the compliance role `srchIndexesAllowed` for `windows`, `auth`, `pam`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 7:** Maintain `nis2_service_accounts.csv` and `nis2_access_review_exceptions.csv` lookups. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 8:** Schedule daily; archive PDF/CSV to `index=audit_evidence`. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 9:** Optional: accelerate the CIM Authentication model for executive roll-ups. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 24 hours, grouped by `sourcetype, EventCode, auth_result, user`. For "NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `sourcetype, EventCode, auth_result, user` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(sourcetype) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.5: NIS2 Network and Information Systems Access Control Audit]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.5,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `Account_Name`, `EventCode`, `Logon_Type`, `after_hours`, `auth_result`, `dest`, `user`. Run:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table Account_Name, EventCode, Logon_Type, after_hours, auth_result, dest, user
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.5: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Timechart Of Failed Logons (`4625`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of After-Hours 4672 Events (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Stacked Bar Of Auth Result By Sourcetype (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Single Value Of Unknown Users Exceeding Threshold (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We keep a single diary that shows who got into our important computer systems, whether they used an extra login check when required, and when someone tried after bedtime. That way we can prove to outsiders we watch the doors properly.
